title: Market Revolution: society and culture
description: Test your skills on how and why innovation in technology, agriculture, and commerce affected various segments of American society over time

assessment_items.question_content:
Year | Number of immigrants 
- | - 
1820 | 8,385
1824 | 7,912
1828 | 27,382
1832 | 60,482
1836 | 76,242 
1840 | 84,066 
1844 | 78,615 
1848 | 226,527

>-Source: U.S. Government Printing Office, [Statistical review of immigration, 1820-1910](https://books.google.com/books/about/Statistical_review_of_immigration_1820_1.html?id=9jGOAAAAMAAJ), 1911 

**The major trend depicted in the graph most directly reflects which of the following developments in the United States during the early nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Large numbers of immigrants from Europe moved to the Northeast to find work in prospering factories
The government sold inexpensive land from the Louisiana Purchase to immigrants
Governmental policies that limited immigration from China after complaints from American workers
The growing need for inexpensive labor to build railroads across the entire continental United States

**Which of the following was a significant cause of the trend from 1820 to 1848 shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Crop losses, lack of economic growth opportunities, and political conflicts in Ireland and Germany
Start of the Russian Civil War, replacing the Russian monarchy with a Communist government
Passage of lax immigration laws that allowed immigrants to relocate to the United States as long as they settled in the West
Series of three major earthquakes in Japan, devastating the countryside and plunging the country into an economic depression

**The migrants represented by the graph most typically settled in which of the following regions of the United States?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Northeast
The Southeast
The Mideast
The Midwest

assessment_items.question_content:
>“For those at the bottom—immigrant and black day laborers, outwork seamstresses, the casual poor—a combination of overstocked labor markets and intense competition among employers kept wages and earnings near or below subsistence levels. Even in New England, farm girls who went off to work in factories expecting decent situations and high wages found that mill conditions had deteriorated by the mid-1830s. Those small independent artisans and well-paid craft workers who survived faced the real possibility of falling into similar distress, victimized as they were by an increasingly volatile business cycle and by the downward pressures on earnings and real wages in various important trades. By the 1830s a new working class was beginning to carve its own identity in a variety of trade unions and in political efforts aimed at redirecting the course and consequences of American economic expansion.”

>-Source: Sean Wilentz, “Society, Politics, and the Market Revolution, 1815-1848,” *The New American History, 1990 

**The development described in the excerpt is most similar to the development in what other period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The period of  innovation during the Industrial Revolution
The introduction of laissez-faire economic policies during the Gilded Age
The push for an economic policy of free silver during the Populist movement
The need for women’s labor for wartime production during World War II

assessment_items.question_content:
>“For those at the bottom—immigrant and black day laborers, outwork seamstresses, the casual poor—a combination of overstocked labor markets and intense competition among employers kept wages and earnings near or below subsistence levels. Even in New England, farm girls who went off to work in factories expecting decent situations and high wages found that mill conditions had deteriorated by the mid-1830s. Those small independent artisans and well-paid craft workers who survived faced the real possibility of falling into similar distress, victimized as they were by an increasingly volatile business cycle and by the downward pressures on earnings and real wages in various important trades. By the 1830s a new working class was beginning to carve its own identity in a variety of trade unions and in political efforts aimed at redirecting the course and consequences of American economic expansion.”

>-Source: Sean Wilentz, “Society, Politics, and the Market Revolution, 1815-1848,” *The New American History, 1990 

**According to the excerpt, which of the following best explains the most significant impact that the Market Revolution had on society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growth of an impoverished class of factory workers
The elimination of a middle class that developed after the American Revolution
The rise in labor union membership to fight for higher wages
The increase in support for the Women’s Rights Movement

assessment_items.question_content:
>“For those at the bottom—immigrant and black day laborers, outwork seamstresses, the casual poor—a combination of overstocked labor markets and intense competition among employers kept wages and earnings near or below subsistence levels. Even in New England, farm girls who went off to work in factories expecting decent situations and high wages found that mill conditions had deteriorated by the mid-1830s. Those small independent artisans and well-paid craft workers who survived faced the real possibility of falling into similar distress, victimized as they were by an increasingly volatile business cycle and by the downward pressures on earnings and real wages in various important trades. By the 1830s a new working class was beginning to carve its own identity in a variety of trade unions and in political efforts aimed at redirecting the course and consequences of American economic expansion.”

>-Source: Sean Wilentz, “Society, Politics, and the Market Revolution, 1815-1848,” *The New American History, 1990 

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Development of low-cost machines that standardized mass production
Surge in emigration from the United States to more developed factory systems in Europe
Rise in immigration from Germany and Ireland to the Midwest
Increase in support for public education, specifically for impoverished working families

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Women's employment, 1820 to 1850** ||
Year | Percentage of women in paid employment | Percentage of total paid workers
- | - | -
1820 | 6.2| 7.3
1830 | 6.4 | 9.6
1840 | 8.4 |9.6
1850 |10.1 |10.8
>-Source: U.S. Census Bureau, *Historical Statistics of the United States, Colonial Times to 1970*, 1975

**Which of the following factors contributed most to the major trend depicted in the table? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The centralization in the production of goods
The commitment towards the education of women
The push for expansion into the Louisiana Territory
The passage of new tariffs, making domestic goods more expensive

**The major trend depicted in the graph most directly reflects which of the following developments in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The transition towards a market economy
The end of the cult of domesticity
The need for labor to meet wartime production goals
The emergence of a women’s rights movement

- | - | -

1840 | 8.4 |9.6

>-Source: U.S. Census Bureau, *Historical Statistics of the United States, Colonial Times to 1970, 1975

**The graph most strongly supports which of the following arguments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Impoverished women were seen as both viable and inexpensive to hire for factory production
Public schools created in the 1830s and 1840s included classes that gave women skills to join factories
Factories increased the number of women they hired because they were cheaper to house
Gender roles changed after the Market Revolution as married women believed it was part of their duty to make money to support their families

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In civil and political affairs, American women take no interest or concern, except so far as they sympathize with their family and personal friends; but in all cases, in which they do feel a concern, their opinions and feelings have a consideration, equal, or even superior, to that of the other sex.

 > “In matters pertaining to the education of their children, in the selection and support of a clergyman, in all benevolent enterprises, and in all questions relating to morals or manners, they have a superior influence. In such concerns, it would be impossible to carry a point, contrary to their judgement [sic] and feelings; while an enterprise, sustained by them, will seldom fail of success.”

>-Source: Catharine Beecher, *A Treatise on Domestic Economy*, 1841

**The excerpt best reflects which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growth in the belief of the separate spheres ideology
The lack of influence of the Second Great Awakening
The rise in women’s participation in the Abolition movement
The surge in women’s participation in the workforce as domestic laborers

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In civil and political affairs, American women take no interest or concern, except so far as they sympathize with their family and personal friends; but in all cases, in which they do feel a concern, their opinions and feelings have a consideration, equal, or even superior, to that of the other sex.

 > “In matters pertaining to the education of their children, in the selection and support of a clergyman, in all benevolent enterprises, and in all questions relating to morals or manners, they have a superior influence. In such concerns, it would be impossible to carry a point, contrary to their judgement [sic] and feelings; while an enterprise, sustained by them, will seldom fail of success.”

>-Source: Catharine Beecher, *A Treatise on Domestic Economy*, 1841

**The views expressed in the excerpt are best seen as evidence of which of the following in American society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Continuities in gender roles for middle class and upper class women  following the Market Revolution
Causes of women’s participation in the workforce increasing during the Market Revolution
Causes of the rise in women’s participation in the Abolition movement after the Second Great Awakening
Changes in the political and voting rights for women following the Seneca Falls Convention
